name: Loren Schoenberg
abstract: Loren Schoenberg (born July 23, 1958) is a tenor saxophonist, conductor, educator, and jazz historian. He has won two Grammy Awards for Best Album Notes.

interviews.title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

quotes.text: sudden I got this bug about oh boy, well all that other stuff was great but boy there’s something about that that really gets me. Well my local public library had a copy of Benny Goodman’s 1938 Carnegie Hall concert, a double LP, which influenced so many people of a certain age. And I fell in love with it. And I just loved listening to it. And at that point I had been taking — I started piano lessons when I was 4. And you know, a typical thing, an old lady who lived on my little street who had like a living room with heavy drapes — I mean it was your typical whatever that experience was you know. You felt like everyone was looking at you and you sat there and you played in the dark drapes and the old lady and the whole thing. But it worked well, you know what I mean? Not interested in jazz at all or anything like that, but I learned basic the Hanon whatever the heck it was.
quotes.caption: Recalling influence of Benny Goodman's 1938 Carnegie Hall album
quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

quotes.text: Right. And John Thompson, “A Day in Bermuda” or whatever they called it. “A snowy Bird” you know, and learned all that stuff. And then, on this Benny Goodman record I listened to it and I looked at all the names and then in my local newspaper, the Bergen Evening Record I saw, I was about, I don’t know, 13 or 14, and it said, “Sundays at the Colonial Post on Main Street in Hackensack, New Jersey, Sundays maybe 5 PM to 8 PM Teddy Wilson.” And I said this cannot be the Teddy Wilson who’s on the Benny Goodman record I’m listening to at Carnegie Hall. I mean what would he be doing playing at an Italian restaurant in Hackensack, New Jersey? Well it turns out that Teddy lived in New Jersey as did Hank Jones, in Bergen County, and when he was around he took whatever gigs he could get just to keep his fingers in shape and maybe he was in between tours or whatever. And so my parents took me to see him and he was very kind and let me sit by the piano and look and all this kind of stuff. I mean I really had no inkling of what I was getting. I mean I knew he was great but all I knew was this one record and that I loved the music. And so my parents got to meet him. And that started a series of things where my parents would meet these musicians, now let’s put it in context. I mean these are African-American musicians. I’m a kid growing up in the 60s and 70s in Bergen County, New Jersey, which was one of the most un-diverse places there could be, although my town, called Fairlawn, was kind of unusual because it had a large Jewish population. On the day that my parents moved in in the 1950s to welcome the first Jewish family on the block everyone put up For Sale signs on all the houses, so they saw that as they drove up the first day. Then they took the signs down. So you know it was not a very diverse place. So what happened was my parents met Teddy Wilson and eventually met Hank Jones and eventually met Eddie Durham and a few other people who were so avuncular and so warm and supportive, my parents said we have no problem at all with our kid leaving him in these hands. Now if you had said to my parents your son’s going © Fillius Jazz Archive -4-
quotes.caption: Mentions hearing Benny Goodman record played at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

quotes.text: know I said oh my God, you know. He went, “Well would you like to come with me.” “Would I like to come with you?” But then how am I going to — my father is going to have to drive back and leave me for three or four hours and then either go back to Fairlawn or go sit somewhere, and I went with Teddy Wilson and I got autographs. It was Lionel Hampton, Gene Krupa, Benny Goodman, Lionel Hampton, Illinois Jacquet played with them, and I think it was George Duvivier or Slam Stewart. And I got all these autographs. And that was the first time that I met Goodman and then when he came to the Jazz Museum I said well I had got your autograph, as though he would remember you know. And he said well maybe you want to come — and I wanted an autographed picture. He said, “Well call my office.” So I called his office and it was on a weekend or something, I went in and it was a woman named Muriel Zuckerman, who had been Benny Goodman’s personal aide for 40 years, like a real Girl Friday kind of thing. She was in love with the boss and a wonderful woman. And I went in and I got this autograph of Benny Goodman, little did I know that I would be running that office several years later. So this is all just giving context to what happened to me before I went to college. Then also in New Jersey there was a jazz workshop at a place called Ramapo College. And it was one of the first I think serious like jazz like summer jazz workshop kind of thing for kids. This was in the early 70s, mid 70s. And it was run by a man named Harold Lieberman, a wonderful trumpet player. He did some studio work. And he had an assistant working for him by the name of Dick Lowenthal. And so they had this wonderful program. And among the faculty were Frankie Dunlop, the drummer who played with Thelonious Monk, Bucky Pizzarelli, Derek Smith, and oh man there was someone else who I just am blanking on, Paul Jeffrey, the saxophone player. And Kenny Washington, I met him there, he was on this thing. And so I was very lucky, when I said about luck, you know because at that time kind of like a burgeoning of jazz education for kids, the Jazz Museum happened to start in New York, Teddy Wilson happened to have a weekend off from playing in Hackensack, New Jersey, and all these things are just coming together. Well I fell in love with the Benny Goodman record at Carnegie Hall, and for whatever reason I zeroed in on this tenor sax solo that Lester Young played on that concert. And I had taken a couple of clarinet lessons, never got very far on it, but at least I learned the fingering. And there was a tenor sax in the high school band room. And I kind of taught myself to play it. I mean it’s not hard to teach yourself the saxophone if you’ve taken a couple of clarinet lessons. I mean it’s very simple fingering and much simpler than the clarinet.
quotes.caption: Recalling early exposure to Benny Goodman at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

quotes.text: let me back up for a moment. I don’t mean we killed it by putting it in the concert hall, that’s a gloss. What I mean to say is that by taking it away from dancing and by taking it away from good times, what Albert Murray writes about in Stomping the Blues and many, many other places, there was a price to be paid. And the price that was paid that it found its place in the concert hall. Now you might ask yourself the question of why do people, especially in America, but why do we behave the way that we behave in the concert hall, listening to classical music? I mean it’s the strangest thing in the world, right? And I won’t go into — I have a long rap about this I do when I’m teaching and I’m sure you do too. But you know the behavior in the concert hall. The opening of the wrapper. The people who are asleep. The people that have no idea what the hell they’re listening to except they feel like they’re eating their spinach and they’re going to see a concert. And they don’t really — the great majority of them I don’t think are that engaged in it. Okay. Now we put jazz in there and take it away from its roots. I think the Ellington band probably had the healthiest diet of Carnegie Hall, colleges, great recordings, dances, dances, dances, dances, parties, parties. Great event. And so the music was informed by all these levels. That’s the beginning of kind of trying to come to terms with what we’re talking about. I’m sorry if I’m a little scattershot here because I’m merging, like you Monk, I’ve been teaching a long time and I have like, you know, these things that I do.
quotes.caption: General commentary on jazz in concert halls
quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

quotes.text: I was gone. My place was taken by Ted Nash. And I’ll say now, you know, thirty-five years, thirty-four years retrospect, Ted’s a better section player than I am you know. But that was a life lesson, you know what I mean? And then Benny died the following June and I mean I kept doing gigs with the band anyway, when I had gigs, and Benny passed away and then I decided soon after there that this was not going to be a Benny Goodman ghost band. I mean (a) his estate eventually said there will be no ghost band. But I really had no desire to go down the road like some other band leaders did. I mean I played Carnegie Hall with Bob Wilber on the Fiftieth Anniversary of the Carnegie Hall concert. I had no desire to go down that (a) I don’t play the clarinet, (b) without Ken Peplowski or a handful of, I mean young guys from my generation back 35 years ago there were not © Fillius Jazz Archive -24-
quotes.caption: Mentioning 1991 Carnegie Hall concert with Bob Wilber
quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Ken Peplowski
about_quotes.evidence: I played in Loren Schoenberg’s band, a great guy who now is up at the Jazz Museum in Harlem. He had a wonderful big band. And we had kind of semi-regular gigs with that band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 9, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Daryl Sherman
about_quotes.evidence: I asked Loren Schoenberg... talked him into doing that with me... He played piano for me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Daryl Sherman interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 11, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Vincent Pelote
about_quotes.evidence: I’ll tell you who has probably very good insight, and you should talk to him if you haven’t already, is Loren Schoenberg at the Jazz Museum in Harlem.
about_quotes.doc_title: Vincent Pelote interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 25, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: James Chirillo
about_quotes.evidence: Ken had a recording project later on where he was able to bring in a big band, and we used Loren Schoenberg’s band and we got it done.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Chirillo interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Tate
about_quotes.evidence: Loren Schoenberg was working for Benny and Loren called me up the next day... He's trying to get me on the band you know
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Tate interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Flanigan
about_quotes.evidence: Well it’s just basically Loren Schoenberg is the source of this story, because he was working for him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Flanigan interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998
